reasoning: On April 7, 2025, HHS Secretary RFK Jr. indicated he would tell the CDC to drop its fluoride recommendation. In response, HHS announced they are reconvening the Community Preventive Services Task Force (CPSTF) to make a "new recommendation" on the issue. As of May 20, 2025 (6 weeks later), the CDC page remains active and explicitly recommends 0.7 mg/L, matching the exact wording of the resolution criteria. Because the administration seems to have opted for a procedural review rather than an immediate executive takedown of the guidance, the timeline becomes the decisive factor. Task force reviews of major public health interventions almost always take several months to a year, as they involve scientific literature reviews and expert panel meetings. Since there are only 41 days until the July 1, 2025 resolution date, the most likely scenario is that the task force will not have completed its review by then, and the existing recommendation will remain on the CDC website pending the final decision. While there is a residual risk (~25%) of a sudden, unilateral withdrawal of the web page by HHS leadership before the task force concludes, the bureaucratic inertia and the lack of such action over the past 6 weeks strongly favor the recommendation surviving past July 1.
